title: A DJ and a Police Investigation
summary: This article is about a DJ named Scott Mills who was investigated by the police. The police looked into claims that he did something wrong with a teenage boy a long time ago, between 1997 and 2000. The boy was under 16 years old. The police started looking in 2016 and talked to Mills in 2018, but they stopped in 2019 because they did not have enough proof to charge him. Recently, the BBC fired Mills from his job, but it is not clear if this is because of the police investigation. The BBC said Mills is no longer working for them. Other DJs are now doing his radio show. The news was first reported by a newspaper called the Mirror.

keywords.explanation: a person who plays music on the radio or at events
when police look into something to find out what happened
a young person who is a boy and between 13 and 19 years old
a big company in the UK that makes TV and radio shows
when someone loses their job because their boss says they cannot work there anymore
evidence or facts that show something is true

What was the age of the teenage boy in the investigation?
When did the police start the investigation?
Why did the police stop the investigation in 2019?
What happened to Scott Mills at the BBC recently?
Who first reported the news about Mills?

background_read: This article talks about a serious topic involving a police investigation into a DJ. It is important to understand that police investigations happen when someone might have broken the law, but sometimes there is not enough evidence to prove it. The BBC is a well-known media company, and when they fire someone, it means that person is no longer allowed to work there. This story shows how news can come from different sources like newspapers and police statements.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scott Mills, a DJ, and the police investigating him.
WHAT: An investigation into claims of wrongdoing with a teenage boy, which ended without charges, and Mills being fired from the BBC.
WHERE: In the UK, involving the Metropolitan Police and the BBC.
WHY: To report on a news story about a public figure and a police case that did not lead to legal action.

perspectives.description: The police investigation shows how the legal system works when there is not enough evidence to charge someone.
The story highlights how news organizations like the BBC and Mirror report on sensitive issues involving celebrities.
